segments.s248-g25: فرستاده را گفت is 'he said to the messenger' — فرستاده is the indirect object and را marks it as the definite/affected object; گفت is past-tense 'said'. رو باز گرد is an imperative phrase: رو (lit. 'face') used with گرد (an archaic/poetic imperative/pooled form of 'turn') plus باز 'back/again' to mean 'turn back' or 'return'.
segments.s248-g26: بگویش is بگو + ـیش (enclitic 'to him'), 'tell him'. که here introduces the reported/quoted words that follow (like 'that' or introducing direct speech), and ای (written attached as که‌ای) is the vocative 'O' addressing the man. خیره ناپاک مرد is a vocative insult: خیره 'dazed/staring', ناپاک 'impure/unholy', مرد 'man'.
segments.s248-g27: تو is the emphatic subject 'you' (poetic/thou). نشنیدی is past/perfective 'did you (not) hear' used rhetorically here. آن داستان پلنگ is a noun phrase 'that tale/story of the leopard' with آن marking definiteness and پلنگ qualifying داستان ('the leopard's tale').
segments.s248-g28: بدان is a contracted/prepositional classical form (ب + آن = 'in/at that'); ژرف دریا is 'the deep sea' with ژرف modifying دریا. که introduces the relative clause 'which struck', and زد (past of زدن) is the simple past 'struck' followed by با نهنگ 'with/against the whale'.
segments.s248-g29: که‌گر (classical compound) = 'if / whoever' introducing a conditional. بر خرد چیره گردد هوا is inverted poetic order: چیره گردد (becomes dominant) is the verb phrase 'becomes victorious/prevails', بر خرد 'over reason' is the phrase it dominates, and هوا (desire/passion) is the subject that prevails; the inversion (verb before subject) is typical in verse.
segments.s248-g30: نیابد here is a present/past-form in classical usage meaning 'does not/ cannot obtain/ find' (negative third person); ز چنگ هوا 'from the claw/grip of desire' uses چنگ metaphorically for 'grip', and کس رها 'no one free' places کس (anyone) before the predicate رها (free), forming 'no one is freed'.
segments.s248-g31: خردمند کرد is 'the wise (one) made/did' — خردمند is agent 'the wise man' and کرد is past 'made/caused'. هوا را (with را marking the definite object) is 'desire' as the object, and بزیر is an adverbial/result ('down'/'to the ground') meaning 'brought down' or 'humbled'. The whole line means 'the wise forced desire down.'
segments.s248-g32: بود is the past copula 'was'. داستانش is 'his story/tale' (داستان + possessive suffix -اش). چو is the comparative 'like/as', and شیر دلیر is the simile 'a brave lion' (noun + postpositive adjective), so 'his tale was like a brave lion.'
segments.s248-g57: بیامد is a past simple verb 'came' placed before the name for poetic inversion. تهمتن is the subject (proper name/epithet). بگسترد is a prefixed past-form (ب + گسترد) meaning 'spread/laid out' and is the second verbal action; بر is the preposition 'upon/on' that waits for its object in the next segment.
segments.s248-g58: بخواهش here functions as a noun-like circumstantial phrase 'at the request' (i.e. 'by the asking'); بر شاه is 'upon the king' with بر = 'on/upon' and شاه = 'king' as the indirect object; خورشید فر is a compound where خورشید is used figuratively as 'sun / splendour' and فر is the technical word for 'royal glory'—together 'sun-like splendour' or 'sun‑splendour' placed upon the king.
segments.s248-g59: ز is the poetic preposition/agent marker 'from' or 'by' used here to introduce the speaker ('by Gorgin'); گرگین is the proper name Gorgin; سخن گفت is the idiomatic combination 'spoke / held a conversation' (سخن = 'speech' + گفت = 'said'); با شهریار = 'with the king' (با = 'with', شهریار = 'king').
segments.s248-g60: ازان = 'of that / about that' (introducing the matter he spoke of). گم‌شده is a past-participial adjective 'lost' modifying بخت. بخت و بد روزگار is a coordinated phrase: بخت = 'fate/fortune', و = 'and', بد روزگار = 'the badness/misfortune of the times' (روزگار = 'time/age'); together 'that lost fortune and the ill fate of the age.'
segments.s248-g61: بدو is the dative/adverbial 'to him' (to that person); گفت is the past 'said' (introducing direct speech). شاه‌ای سپهدار من is a vocative phrase: the king is addressing the addressee as 'O, my sepahdar/commander' (شاه‌ای here marks the speech from the Shah and سپهدار من = 'my commander'). Note word order is poetic/vocative.
segments.s248-g62: همی is an adverb 'always / continually' (a frequentive poetic particle). بگسلی is related to the root گسل/گسست 'to break, loosen' (here verbal notion 'break/loosen'); بند is 'fetter/bond' (object). و links clauses, and زنهار من is 'my warning' or an imperative 'take heed (my warning)'—زنهار can be interjectional 'beware!' or a noun 'a warning'; here it functions as the king's admonition. The personal من attaches as 'my' in 'سپهدار من' earlier and as the source of the warning here.
segments.s248-g63: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of what is sworn. سوگند خوردم is an idiom: خوردم with سوگند here means 'I took/swore an oath' (literally 'I ate an oath' in older idiom). بتخت و کلاه is a poetic compacting: b- prefix + تخت = 'by/at the throne' and کلاه 'crown' (kalaah used metonymically for crown); together 'by the throne and the crown'—an oath-formula.
segments.s248-g64: بدارای is a noun/nominal form meaning 'possessions / that which is possessed' (used in an oath). بهرام is the proper name Bahram. The coordinated خورشید و ماه 'sun and moon' are further oath-terms; و are simple coordinators. The whole segment lists sacred/powerful things by which the oath is sworn.
segments.s248-g105: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/for' introducing the two substantivized participles. گستردنی and پوشیدنی are deverbal adjectives used as nouns ('that which is spread' / 'that which is worn' or 'spreadables' / 'wearables'); هم between them means 'also/both' linking the two items. Word order is preposition + noun; repetition of ز before پوشیدنی emphasizes both categories.
segments.s248-g106: بباید is an impersonal/modal old literary form meaning 'it must / it is necessary' (literally 'one must'). It governs two nominal complements joined by و: بهایی ('a price; value') and بخشیدنی (a participle meaning 'to be given/grantable'). The structure is 'it must be price and (it must be) give‑able,' i.e. they should have a price and be distributable.
segments.s248-g107: چو here means 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' and appears before the subject (خسرو) in inverted, poetic word order: 'when heard Khosrow...'. ز رستم سخن is a prepositional phrase 'speech/words from Rostam' = 'Rostam's words'.
segments.s248-g108: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces the intended result/clause here ('that/so that'), leading into the object of the order (گنجهای کهن). گنجهای is the plural 'treasures/treasuries' and کهن 'ancient', forming the noun phrase that the king ordered about.
segments.s248-g109: همه پاک is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'entirely/wholly' (همه + پاک used adverbially). بگشاد is an archaic past form 'opened' (prefix بـ + گشاد) and takes گنجور ('treasury, treasure-house') as its object. شاه at the end is the subject; poetic word order can place the subject after verb and object.
segments.s248-g110: بدینار is an adverbial/phrasing 'with dinars' (literary form built on دینار). و links گوهر ('gems, jewels') as another instrument/thing used. بیاراست is the past verb 'he adorned/arranged'. گاه is a temporal/occasional word ('time/occasion') closing the clause; here it marks the occasion or simply 'then'.
segments.s248-g111: تهمتن (Tahmatan) is the proper name/epithet. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. همه بنگرید is literally 'looked at all' — همه here functions as 'everything' and بنگرید is the past/perfective gaze verb (poetic form of 'to look'), so together 'he looked over everything.'
segments.s248-g112: هر آنچش is a compound meaning 'whichever/whatever (that which)'. ببایست is the impersonal/modal past form 'was necessary/fit' (here 'that he should/had need to'), and زان 'from it' indicates source. برگزید is simple past 'chose/selected'. The line reads 'he chose from it whatever he ought/needed.'
segments.s248-g129: پس is a temporal adverb 'then'. آگاهی is the subject meaning 'news/information' and آمد is the simple past 3rd‑person singular 'came'. بگردنکشان is a prepositional phrase (ب = to) attached to گردنکشان ('the high‑necked', i.e. the proud), so the whole segment means 'then news came to the proud.'
segments.s248-g130: بدان here functions as a single unit meaning 'about that' / 'concerning that' (built from ب + آن in older usage) and introduces the following nominal phrase. گرزداران دشمن کشان is a noun phrase in which گرزداران ('mace‑bearers') is followed by a descriptive compound دشمن‌کشان: دشمن (enemy) + کشان, the productive suffix/participle element forming an agentive/‑slaying sense, so 'enemy‑slayers' or 'slayers of enemies.' The phrase therefore reads 'about those mace‑bearers, the slayers of enemies.'
segments.s248-g131: بپرسید is past 3rd‑person singular 'asked' (classical narrative past). زنگه is the person addressed (a proper name) and که introduces the subordinate interrogative clause. خسرو کجاست uses کجاست (کجا + ست) with ست as the reduced classical copula 'is', so 'where Khosrow is/was.'
segments.s248-g132: چه is the interrogative 'what'. آمد is past 3rd‑person 'came/occurred'. برویش literally 'to/onto him' (ب + روی + ـش) yields the idiom آمد برویش 'what came upon him' = 'what befell him'. که introduces the result/clause 'that', ما را is the object 'us' with the definite/emphatic marker را, and بخواست is past 3rd‑person 'he desired/wanted' (here meaning 'he summoned/wished for our presence').
segments.s248-g1: چو is the archaic/poetic conditional/temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause follows Persian SOV order: گرگین نشان (object, 'Gorgin's sign') + تهمتن (subject, 'Tahmētan') + شنید (verb, 'heard/received'). Thus the whole segment means 'when Tahmētan heard Gorgin's sign'.
segments.s248-g2: بدانست is a past-tense, compact form meaning 'he knew it / he perceived'. The following clause is reported content introduced elliptically by the old-style conjunctional contraction کآمد (که آمد = 'that came/arrived'); غمش را (object marked by را) precedes its predicate کلید ('key' used figuratively). The literal order yields 'he knew that the key to his grief had come.'
segments.s248-g3: فرستاد is a simple past verb 'he sent/dispatched'. The phrase نزدیک رستم is an adverbial/prepositional-like phrase placed after the verb (poetic order) meaning 'near Rostam' or 'to Rostam's vicinity'. پیام is the direct object 'a message'.
segments.s248-g4: که‌ای here functions as an archaic clause introducer ('that' / introducing reported speech or content). تیغ بخت و وفا را نیام is a noun phrase in which تیغ بخت و وفا ('the sword of fortune and fidelity') is linked to نیام ('scabbard, sheath'); را marks the phrase as definite/foregrounded (object/topic). Grammatically this segment names the scabbard of that sword as the content of the message (the verb for what is said is elliptical or continues in the next line).
segments.s248-g5: This is a stacked nominal phrase: درخت بزرگی uses the noun + following noun-as-attribute pattern ('a tree of greatness' = 'a great tree'), and گنج وفا likewise ('the treasure of fidelity'). The conjunction و links the two parallel noun phrases.
segments.s248-g6: در is the preposition 'in' governing both coordinated nouns that follow (رادمردی and بند بلا). رادمردی is an abstract noun 'magnanimity' and بند بلا is a compound where بند ('bond/fetter') is modified by بلا ('calamity/affliction'), together meaning 'the bonds of affliction'. The preposition applies to both items joined by و.
segments.s248-g7: گرت is an archaic contraction equivalent to 'اگر تو' ('if you'). رنج ناید is a negative-stative construction: ناید here means 'does not come' (i.e., 'no offense/sorrow comes'). ز گفتار من uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from' or 'because of' to mark the source 'from my speech'. Together: 'If no offense comes from my speech (i.e., if you are not offended by what I say)'.
segments.s248-g8: سخن گسترانی is an exhortative/verbal-nominal phrase: سخن ('speech/praise') + گسترانی (verbal stem meaning 'to spread/extend') — here understood as an instruction 'spread words' or 'speak broadly'. ز کردار من is a prepositional phrase 'about/from my deeds' (ز = 'concerning' / 'from'). The whole segment = 'spread (speak) words about my deeds.'
segments.s248-g81: This is a conditional clause introduced by اگر (if). شاه (king) is the subject, بیند is a present/subjunctive-form of دید 'to see' used here as 'sees' or 'should see', and بمن is the enclitic prepositional phrase 'to me'. بخشدش is a compound verb with the clitic -ش (him); here it means 'give/grant him' (i.e., hand him over). Word order is Subject–Verb–IndirectObject in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s248-g82: مگر here introduces the exception 'unless'. اختر نیک is a two‑word noun phrase 'auspicious/fortunate star' (نیک = auspicious here), and بدرخشدش is the verb بدرخشیدن with the clitic -ش 'shine upon him'. The verb is in a present/subjunctive/optative sense 'shine upon/turn favorably to him'.
segments.s248-g83: This clause is compressed/poetic in word order. برستم here functions as the name 'Rostam' with an archaic/prosthetic b- (the supplied lemma senses do not fit the proper-name reading, so -1 is used). ببخشید is a past verb 'pardoned/forgave' (or 'gave/bestowed' in other contexts) and پیروز شاه is an appositive noun phrase 'the victorious king' that follows; in poetry subjects and objects may appear in flexible order.
segments.s248-g84: رهانیدش is past tense 'released/rescued him' with the clitic -ش. از introduces the source 'from', بند means 'fetter, bond', و = 'and', تاریک = 'dark', and چاه = 'pit/well'. Together: 'released him from the fetters and the dark pit.' Word order here is Verb–Object–Prepositional phrase, typical of poetry.
segments.s248-g85: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here marking the person asked). رستم is the addressee/name. بپرسید is a past/formal verb 'asked' (third person), پس = 'then', and شهریار = 'the king' (subject). The sequence places the object/target (ز رستم) before the verb and the subject (شهریار) after, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s248-g86: This is an indirect question introduced by که ...; چون is used as 'how', راند is past or aorist stem meaning 'drive/put to flight' or more generally 'execute/perform', and خواهی is the second‑person future/auxiliary 'you will' (here embedded in reported speech). بر + ین (برین = 'this/that upon') + گونه 'manner' + کار 'deed' form the noun phrase 'in this manner of action' — so the whole clause asks 'how will you drive/perform this sort of deed?'.
segments.s248-g87: چه باید is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what must (be done)'; ز is 'from', گنج = 'treasure', و = 'and', زلشکر is a compound noun (textually obscure; here treated as a store/treasury item as given), and بخواه is the imperative/subjunctive 'ask/demand' (second person). The clause instructs to demand from these resources.
segments.s248-g88: This is a cleft/explanatory clause introduced by که 'that'. باید که = 'it is necessary that', با = 'with', تو = 'you', بیاید is a present/subjunctive/optative 'may come' or 'come' (third person), and براه = 'on the road/along the way'. Together: 'that it is necessary that he come with you on the road.'
segments.s248-g33: نبایدش is the impersonal negative modal نباید + enclitic ش (referring back to the action); it means 'it is not right / one should not'. بردن is the infinitive 'to take/carry off' and directly follows the modal. بنخچیر is the object 'spoil, prey', and روی here is adverbial 'in sight / openly' placed after the verb phrase.
segments.s248-g34: نه نیز is a negative additive ('nor/also not'). از introduces the source ('from / by'). رنجش آید is an impersonal construction 'resentment/sorrow comes' (آید 'comes' used impersonally). The remaining lexical items appear in an archaic or dialectal form here (see senses). The whole clause says that resentment from the robbers will come upon you.
segments.s248-g35: تو is the 2nd‑person singular subject 'you'. دستان here is literal 'hands' (gestures), and نمودی is the simple past 'you showed'. چو introduces a simile 'like', and روباه پیر is the noun + adjective 'old fox' (noun followed by adjective, common in classical Persian).
segments.s248-g36: ندیدی is the past negative 'you did not see'. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here ('ever/at all'). دام is 'trap' and نخچیرگیر is a compound meaning 'hunter/one who takes spoils' (object follows the verb in normal Persian order).
segments.s248-g37: نشاید is an impersonal negative verbal adjective meaning 'it is not fitting / it ought not'. کزین is a fused archaic prepositional phrase 'from this' (کز + این). بیهده کام is a noun phrase 'futile / senseless desire' with کام 'desire' and the enclitic تو marking possession 'yours'.
segments.s248-g38: که is the complementizer 'that'. من is the subject 'I'. پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' (پیشِ کسی). خسرو is the king's name. برم is the 1st‑person form of بردن used here in a subjunctive/optative sense 'that I should bring/mention'; نام تو 'your name' is the object (i.e. to speak/bring up your name before the king).
segments.s248-g39: ولیکن is the adversative conjunction 'but'. چو here functions temporally 'when / now that'. اکنون 'now' specifies time, and ببیچارگی is an abstract noun 'in wretchedness / helplessness' describing the state.
segments.s248-g40: فرو is an adverb/prefix meaning 'down / deeply'. مانده is a participial/predicative form 'remained / fallen (and stayed)'. گشتی here is an auxiliary/result verb meaning 'you have become / you are made' in classical usage (note: the lemma senses given in the data do not reflect this verbal use). بیکبارگی is an adverb 'suddenly', placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s248-g89: بترسم is first‑person present (b‑ prefix + root) meaning “I fear.” ز is the poetic preposition “because/of” introducing the reason. بد گوهر is a noun phrase where بد modifies گوهر as “evil/ill essence” (i.e. evil nature) and افراسیاب is the proper name; the whole phrase means “because of Afrasiab’s evil nature.”
segments.s248-g90: که is the subordinating particle “that” introducing the content clause. بر جان بیژن is a prepositional phrase “upon Bijan’s life” (بر + object). بگیرد is a present/subjunctive form with b‑ prefix meaning “seize/take” and شتاب is a noun “haste” used adverbially — together بگیرد شتاب = “seize/take (his) life quickly.”
segments.s248-g91: یکی is the indefinite “one / a certain.” بادسارست is an obscure epithet/name placed before دیو (demon) and should be read as a modifier or name; دیو نژند is a noun + adjective “an ignoble/base demon.” The phrase as a whole introduces “a certain (named) base demon.”
segments.s248-g92: بسی is an adverb “much / many / greatly.” خوانده here is a perfect/participial use meaning “learned / has recited” (describing capacity). افسون و نیرنگ و بند is a coordinated list of objects (“charms, trickery/deceptions, and bonds/fetters”) governed by خوانده, so the whole segment means he was much versed in/ had learned many spells, deceits and bonds. Note بند here means a binding/fetter (magical or physical).
segments.s248-g93: بجنباندش is a causative present form (b‑ + root) with the enclitic object ‑ش “him/it,” meaning “(he) causes him/it to move / stirs him.” اهرمن is the agent (Ahriman), دل is the affected noun “heart,” and ز جای is the prepositional phrase “from its place.” Word order is compact/classical: verb + clitic object + agent + patient + prepositional phrase.
segments.s248-g94: بیندازد is another causative/active present (be‑ + اندازد) meaning “he causes to throw / casts down.” آن تیغ‌زن را is the definite object marked by را (“that swordsman”), and زپای is an adverbial “from/off his feet.” The clause says the agent casts that swordsman down off his feet.
segments.s248-g95: چنین is a formulaic adverb “thus/so.” گفت here is the verb “said,” but the provided senses list does not include the verbal sense “to say” (see note). رستم is the speaker and بشاه جهان is the indirect object “to the king of the world” (بشاه = to the king / O king). The phrase is a conventional lead‑in: “Thus said Rostam to the King of the World.”
segments.s248-g96: که is the subordinating particle “that.” این کار is “this matter/affair.” ببسیچم is an archaic/obscure verbal form in the text (the provided analysis labels it uncertain); functionally it expresses Rostam’s intent “I will do/handle” (perform or carry out). اندر نهان is the adverbial phrase “in secret/within secrecy.” Together the clause means he will carry out this deed secretly.
segments.s248-g73: گر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). آمرزش شاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's pardon' (noun + possessor). نایدش is an archaic/poetic negative form of آمدن ('does not come') with the enclitic -ش 'to him' attached, so the verb phrase means 'does not come to him'. پیش is an adverb 'before/in advance' placed after the verb for emphasis ('beforehand').
segments.s248-g74: نبودیش is a nominal/verbal construction meaning 'there is not for him' / 'he will lack' (here indicating lack or absence). نام ('name') is used in the sense 'reputation, fame'. و simply links clauses ('and'). برآید is a present/future-oriented intransitive 'arise/appear' (the thing will come to be). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and کیش here means 'character/custom/way' (i.e., his manner or inner nature), so the clause says his name will be absent and his character will come to light.
segments.s248-g75: هرآن کس que 'whoever / any person who' begins the relative clause. که گردَد is the relative clause marker + verb; گردَد (from گردیدن/گشتن) here means 'turns/strays' (i.e., 'turns away'). ز راه خرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the path of wisdom' (ز = 'from', راه = 'path', خرد = 'wisdom'), so the clause literally 'anyone who turns from the path of wisdom...'.
segments.s248-g76: سرانجام is an adverbial 'in the end / finally'. پیچد (from پیچیدن) here carries the sense of 'become entangled/come to grief' or 'be brought low'—an intransitive/resultative event. ز marks the cause or source 'because of / from' and کردار خود means 'by his own deeds' (کردار = deeds; خود = 'own' reflexive), so the clause says the final outcome is caused by his own actions.
segments.s248-g77: سزد‌گر is a fixed/poetic conditional expression meaning 'if it is fitting' (literally 'fitting/if one deems it fit'). کنی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن 'you do/you should do', so together 'if you deem it fitting to do/recall...'. یاد کردار اوی places یاد ('remembrance/recall') before the phrase 'کردار اوی' ('his deeds' with enclitic اوی = 'his'), functioning as the object: 'to remember his deeds'.
segments.s248-g78: همیشه is the adverb 'always'. بهر introduces purpose or motive 'for / on account of' and is followed by کینه 'hatred / grudge' as the reason. پیکار اوی is a nominal predicate 'his battle / he is at battle' with اوی ('his' or 'he') as a pronominal suffix; Persian often omits a copula, so the clause means 'he is always at strife from hatred' (i.e., he always fights out of spite).
segments.s248-g79: بپیش is a prepositional/adverbial 'in the presence of / before'. نیاکانت is the possessive plural 'your ancestors' (نیاکان + enclitic -ت). بسته is a past participle/adjectival 'tied / girded' and کمر ('waist / belt') completes the idiom بستن کمر 'to gird one's loins'—so the phrase orders 'gird your loins before your ancestors' (imperative sense implied).
segments.s248-g80: بهر کینه is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of hatred / for revenge'. گه here is an adverb 'occasionally / at times' modifying the following clause. با یکی کینه ور is 'with a certain (یکی) grudging / vengeful person' (کینه‌ور is a compound meaning 'one bearing grudge'); note that ور as an adjectival/derivational element ('-bearing') is poetic/archaic. The whole second hemistich says 'occasionally, for the sake of vengeance, be allied with a certain grudging man' (word order reflects poetic inversion).
segments.s248-g97: This is an inverted copular clause: کلید … باشد فریب = “the key is deception.” چنین بند (literally “such bond/tie”) modifies کلید and sets the domain (“to such a matter”). باشد is the classical third‑person copula/optative form used here as “is.”
segments.s248-g98: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning “one must not / it must not be done.” برین کار کردن = بر + این + کار + کردن (literally “in doing this work”/“while performing this deed”), where کردن is the verbal noun/infinitive “to do.” نهیب is the affected noun “a shout/cry” (here a war‑cry); the whole yields “one must not cry out while doing this.”
segments.s248-g99: نه هنگام = “it is not the time” (نه negates هنگام “time/opportunity”). گرزست is the poetic fused form of گرز است “(the) mace” and here functions as an item in a series: گرز (mace) و تیغ (sword) و سنان (spear). The list names weapons that are unsuitable now.
segments.s248-g100: بدین کار = به + این + کار “for/to this work”; in poetry the components can appear fused. باید is the impersonal modal “it is necessary / one must,” followed by the infinitive/gerund کشیدن “to pull/draw.” عنان (“reins”) is the object of کشیدن; کشیدن عنان is an idiom meaning “to restrain / hold in (literally ‘draw the reins’).”
segments.s248-g101: This nominal clause states requirements: فراوان گهر باید = “many/ plentiful gems are necessary.” The coordinating و links زرو سیم (gold and silver) as additional required valuables; زرو is an archaic/poetic form related to زر “gold.”
segments.s248-g102: This gives behavioral rules using infinitival/nominal forms: برفتن here means “to go forth / when departing,” followed by the predicate پر امید “full of hope.” The contrast و بودن به بیم uses بودن (to be) with the prepositional phrase به بیم “in fear,” so: “When departing be hopeful, and when staying be fearful.”
segments.s248-g103: بکردار بازارگانان شدن = becom(e) of merchant‑conduct / “adopt the conduct of merchants.” بکردار is an adjective/epithet meaning ‘of behavior/conduct’ or ‘well‑behaved’; بازارگانان is the plural ‘merchants’; شدن is the verb ‘to become’ forming the infinitival idea “to become/have the conduct of merchants.”
segments.s248-g104: This continues the advice: شکیبا فراوان = “very patient / abundant patience.” بتوران is the fused prepositional phrase به توران “to Turan,” and بدن here functions as an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning “send/give (them)” (i.e. have many sent into Turan). The idiom reads: be very patient and send many into Turan. (بدن is an archaic/poetic verbal form not matched by the simple noun senses listed.)
segments.s248-g65: که here introduces a wish/curse clause (an optative use: 'may it be that...'). گرگین is the proper name 'Gorgin' as the clause's subject. نبیند is the present-subjunctive/optative form of دیدن with negative prefix ن‑; together with جز ('except') it yields the sense 'see nothing except...'. ز من is the poetic preposition 'from me' (indicating source), and جز بلا literally 'except calamity' — i.e. 'nothing but calamity.'
segments.s248-g66: مگر here means 'unless'. بیژن is the subject 'Bijan'. از بند is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) bond(s)/fetters'. یابد is the present/subjunctive of یافتن 'to find/obtain' used here jussively ('may he find / may he obtain'), and رها is an adjectival/predicative element 'free'/'released' — together 'may Bijan be found free from his bonds.'
segments.s248-g67: جزین = 'except this / apart from this' (limits what follows). آرزو is 'wish' (the thing excepted). هرچ modifies the verb and means 'whatever'; باید is a modal 'it is necessary / you should'; بخواه is the present-subjunctive/imperative form of خواستن 'to ask/seek' — together: 'apart from this wish, ask for whatever you should / whatever you want.'
segments.s248-g68: A simple list introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from'. تخت 'throne' and مهر here mean 'favour/affection' (requests one might demand), تیغ 'sword' (military power), and کلاه 'hat/helmet' (here wearing a helmet/war‑gear). The repeated ز + و links the items in a poetic enumerative rhythm: 'from throne and from favour and from sword and (from) helmet.'
segments.s248-g69: پس آنگه چنین is a stacked temporal/contrastive phrase 'then / thereupon / thus'. گفت is the verb 'said' (here placed before the name in the conventional epic order 'thus said Rostam'), رستم is the speaker's name, and بشاه is an old-form prepositional/vocative 'to the king' / 'O king' (here marking the addressee).
segments.s248-g70: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory compound (که + ای) addressing the king — 'O you who are...'. پرهنر 'full of skill / accomplished' and نامور 'renowned' are epithets modifying the addressee, and پیشگاه literally 'presence' (here 'in the royal presence' or 'before the court'), a formal honorific location. Grammatically this is a vocative phrase directed at the king.
segments.s248-g71: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. بد is an adverb/adjective 'bad/evil' qualifying سگالید; سگالید is the poetic/archaic verb 'to set dogs on / to harry' (i.e. to attack with hunting‑dogs or to assail). پیچد is the present form 'turns/comes about' (here close to 'will ensue' or 'will turn out') and همی is the older continuous/emphatic prefix/particle ('indeed / does so / habitually'), so the clause says 'if he assails (with bad dogs) / if such an attack occurs...'.
segments.s248-g72: فدا کردن is the compound verbal expression 'to sacrifice' (فدا + کردن), and جان is the object 'life'. بسیچد is an archaic/poetic verbal form (built on بسی‑ 'many' + a verb stem) that here functions as a verbal modifier meaning 'do abundantly / do repeatedly' (i.e. 'will do much / will do many times'); همی again gives the continuous/emphatic aspect. Together: 'he will abundantly/repeatedly sacrifice his life' — i.e. 'he will repeatedly offer his life in sacrifice.'
segments.s248-g113: ازان (azān) is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'from that / thereupon' placed before the noun phrase. صد شتر is a numeral + noun ('a hundred camels') with the numeral preceding the noun. بار دینار کرد uses بار (load) + اسم (دینار) and the light verb کرد (past of کردن) to mean 'made/caused to carry a load of dinars'—کرد is the simple past main verb applying to the whole preceding noun phrase.
segments.s248-g114: صد اشتر repeats the idea 'a hundred camels' (here spelled slightly differently). ز گنج درم بار کرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the treasure (ز گنج) [he] loaded with dirhams (درم بار کرد)'. Again بار + کرد functions as 'to load/cause to bear a cargo', with کرد the past-tense light verb closing the clause.
segments.s248-g115: بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb 'he commanded/said'. رستم names the speaker. بسالار is سالار (commander/chief) with the prefixed بـ marking address/target ('to the chief' / 'the chief of the packs'); بار here follows as the object of the order context—'the baggage/load (for the chief to handle)'. The sequence is subject/verb/name + indirect object.
segments.s248-g116: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that') and is followed by بگزین, an imperative/subjunctive form of گزینیدن 'choose' (here an instruction). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', followed by گردان لشکر 'the regiments/troops of the army' (گردان modifies لشکر). The clause ends with the numeral implied (هزار) — the whole reads 'that (you) choose from the regiments a thousand.'
segments.s248-g117: ز here is the preposition 'from'. مردان is the plural noun 'men' (here 'men/warriors' / 'heroes'). گردنکش and نامور are two adjectives joined by و: گردنکش 'headstrong/rebellious' and نامور 'famous/renowned', both qualifying مردان. Word order is preposition + modified noun phrase.
segments.s248-g118: بباید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / one must' (present/future sense 'must/ought to'). تنی چند is an idiom meaning 'a few / several' (تنی here 'a body/one person' used as 'some'), and بسته کمر is a compound participial phrase 'having girded the loins / with girdles fastened' (بسته as 'tied/bound' modifying کمر 'waist / belt'), so the clause means 'several must gird their waists' (i.e. prepare themselves).
segments.s248-g119: چو/چون are comparative particles 'as/like'. The sequence lists names or comparisons: گرگین (here a proper name, Gorgin) and زنگه (a name in context) followed by شاوران which, in form, can mean 'advisers' or a group name. Grammatically this is a series of similative comparisons ('such as Gorgin and Zangah, (and) the Shavaran').
segments.s248-g120: دگر is 'other / another'. گستهم here functions as a proper name in the list (though its listed sense is different in the lexicon); شیر is a noun 'lion' used metaphorically, and جنگ آوران is a compound 'war-bringers' / 'warriors' (جنگ 'war' + آوران 'bringers/bearers'). Together this segment lists other heroes: 'others — Gostaham, lions, bringers of war.'
segments.s248-g41: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the person addressed ('از خسرو' = 'from Khosrow'). 'بخواهم' is first-person present/subjunctive of خواستن here meaning 'I will ask/request' (classical usage: از X بخواهم Y = 'I will ask X for Y'). 'گناه ترا' is the thing being requested — 'گناه' as 'blame/fault' with the enclitic 'ت' marking 'you' (ترا = 'you' as object). The final 'را' is the definite-object/topic marker attached to the phrase.
segments.s248-g42: بیفروزم is a first-person optative/jussive of افروز ('may I brighten' / 'I will brighten'); here it expresses an entreaty/hope. The following noun phrase 'این تیره ماه ترا' is the object: 'این' = 'this', 'تیره' = 'dark', 'ماه' = 'moon' (poetic for a face/beautiful one), and 'ترا' again combines the 2nd-person clitic -ت plus the object marker را to mean 'your (moon) / you' as the possessed. Word order places the verb before the object for emphasis.
segments.s248-g43: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. 'بیژن' is the subject (the hero Bijan). 'از بند' = 'from the bond(s)'; بند = 'fetter/bond'. 'یابد' here is a 3rd-person subjunctive/optative form of یافتن used to mean 'become/obtain' in the sense 'is set/free' (classical idiom: یابدِ رها = 'is freed'). 'رها' functions as a predicate/adjectival complement 'free, released.'
segments.s248-g44: بفرمان is an ablative/agentive prepositional phrase 'by the order/command (of)'. The sequence 'دادار گیهان خدا' is an appositive/title construction: 'دادار گیهان' = 'the Creator/Giver of the world' and 'خدا' = 'God' clarifies the referent. The whole phrase qualifies the conditional clause as being under divine command.
segments.s248-g45: رهاگشتی is a second-person past/perfect form meaning 'you became free / you were freed'. 'از بند' again 'from the bond(s)'. The conjunction و links the clauses. 'رستی' is the second-person past of رستن meaning 'you were saved/escaped' (i.e. you escaped alive). 'بجان' is a prepositional phrase literally 'by the life' used idiomatically 'with your life' (i.e. alive).
segments.s248-g46: ز is the preposition 'from'. 'تو' is the second-person pronoun 'you'. 'دور شد' (دور + شد) is a past/perfect verbal phrase 'became distant / moved away'. The noun phrase 'کینه بدگمان' is the subject that moved away: 'کینه' = 'grudge/hatred' modified by the adjectival compound 'بدگمان' = 'suspicious / full of doubt'.
segments.s248-g47: وگر = 'and if / but if'. جز here means 'other than' or 'elsewhere'. The sequence 'برین' is an archaic/poetic grouping corresponding to 'بر این' = 'on/at this'; combined with 'روی' ('face') it yields 'elsewhere than this face'. 'گردد' is the 3rd-person subjunctive/present of گردیدن meaning 'turns / becomes'. 'سپهر' ('heavens/sky') is the logical subject: 'if the sky turns its face away from this.'
segments.s248-g48: ز introduces the source 'from'. 'جان و تن' = 'life (soul) and body'. 'خوی' + 'ش' together form the possessive 'خویش' ('his/her/one's own'); here they function as 'your (own)' referring back to the addressee. 'بردار' is the imperative 'take away / remove'. 'مهر' is the object meaning 'love/affection'. Together: 'remove love from your life and body.'
segments.s248-g121: The phrase begins with the ordinal 'چهارم' ('the fourth') followed by a name (گرازه) in apposition. 'که' introduces a relative clause ('who/that'), and 'راند' is a past-tense verb meaning 'drove/put to flight', with its object 'سپاه' ('a host/army') following it.
segments.s248-g122: 'فروهل' is another name in apposition; 'نگهبان' is a noun meaning 'guardian/keeper' and takes the following genitives 'تخت و کلاه' ('throne and helmet') joined by 'و'. Word order is noun (name) + role + objects.
segments.s248-g123: 'چو' here is the comparative/simile 'like'. 'فرهاد و رهام' are coordinated proper names. The phrase ends with 'گرد دلیر': 'گرد' literally means 'around/about' but in this poetic collocation functions adverbially/qualifying 'دلیر' to give a compact sense of being stout/brave — i.e. 'brave like...'.
segments.s248-g124: Again 'چو' = 'like'. 'اشکش' here functions as a proper name (hence treated as a name, not the common noun 'tear'); 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who'. 'صید' is the object 'prey', placed before the verb 'آورد' ('brought/captured'), and 'نره شیر' is a noun phrase 'male lion' with 'نره' specifying 'male'.
segments.s248-g125: 'چنین' = 'such/thus' modifies the following noun 'هفت یل' ('seven heroes'). 'باید' is an impersonal/modal auxiliary meaning 'must/ought to', and 'آراسته' is a past-participle/adjective 'adorned/equipped' used predicatively with the modal ('must be equipped').
segments.s248-g126: 'نگهبان' is the noun 'guardian/warder' and takes the genitive phrase 'این لشکر' ('this army') that follows it; 'و خواسته' is appended in coordination — here 'خواسته' functions adjectivally/nominally as 'chosen/desired', producing 'guardians and chosen (men) of this host' (a compact poetic expression where خواسته qualifies the role).
segments.s248-g127: 'همه' ('all/everyone') is the subject; 'تاج و زیور' are the coordinated direct objects ('crown and ornament/jewelry'); 'بینداختند' is past-tense plural 'they threw off/removed' (an older/poetic form corresponding to ان‌داختند/انداختند).
segments.s248-g128: 'چنانچون' = 'just as/so that' links to what follows; 'ببایست' is an impersonal/modal form meaning 'it was necessary/it was proper', and 'برساختند' is past plural 'they constructed/made/arranged' — here idiomatically 'they prepared themselves' or 'they set themselves up' as fitting the situation.
segments.s248-g49: نخستین (an emphatic adjective) precedes the subject pronoun من for focus: 'first I...'. آیم is the archaic/polite present-future form of آمدن ('I come' / 'I shall come') used as a finite verb. بدین here is the fused preposition phrase به + این ('by/for this') linking the following role کینه‌خواه ('the avenger'); overall the clause says 'I come first as the avenger in/for this (matter)'.
segments.s248-g50: بنیروی is a prepositional phrase (بنیروی = به+نیروی) meaning 'by the power/virtue of' and governs the following genitives. یزدان and فرمان شاه are coordinated sources: 'by God's power and by the king's command' (فرمان is 'command/edict', شاه modifies فرمان). The conjunction و simply links the two authorities.
segments.s248-g51: وگر introduces a conditional 'if'. من نیایم uses the negative or prohibitive form of آیم (نیا + -یم) meaning 'if I do not come' (نیایم here = 'do not come / should not come'). چو introduces a comparison/temporal link 'as/if/when' connecting the following names (گودرز و گیو) as the agents who will act in that case.
segments.s248-g52: بخواهد is the finite verb (here future/optative sense 'will desire/seek/exact'); it carries the force for the whole clause. ز تو is the prepositional object 'from you'. کینه is the thing sought ('vengeance, grudge'), and the remaining sequence (پور نیو) appears as a patronymic or name in apposition (addressing the addressee); classical Persian often uses a singular verb form even when multiple agents are implied.
segments.s248-g53: برآمد is a past/punctual verb 'arose/occurred'. بر + ین is the fused prepositional phrase بر این ('upon/with this') modifying کار ('the affair/deed'). یک روز و شب is an adverbial duration 'one day and (one) night' placed after the verbal clause, a common poetic order.
segments.s248-g54: و introduces the next clause. زین here is the fused form of از + این ('from/at this'); گفته is the noun/participle 'said/report' so زِ این گفته = 'from/at this saying/report'. بر شاه is the prepositional phrase 'to/at the king' (بر used as dative). نگشاد is the past negative of گشودن ('did not open'), and لب ('lip') is the object: 'the king did not open his lips.'
segments.s248-g55: دوم روز is the temporal adverbial 'on the second day'. چون introduces a temporal clause 'when/as', and شاه is the subject. بنمود is a past transitive verb 'showed/displayed/put on' taking تاج ('the crown') as its direct object; the clause means 'when the king displayed the crown.'
segments.s248-g56: نشست is the past tense 'he sat' (or 'he took his seat'). از بر is an archaic/prepositional combination equivalent to 'on/upon' here; سیمگون is an adjective 'silvery' modifying تخت ('throne'), and عاج ('ivory') further specifies the material of the throne. The phrase order (adjective before تخت and material after) is typical of classical Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s248-g17: مرا‌گر is an inversion of اگر مرا (’if me/if you want me’) with the object pronoun مرا fronted for emphasis; بخواهی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive used in a conditional sense (‘if you want’). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ and شاه جوان is a noun + adjective sequence (‘the young king’) with the adjective following the noun (normal in Persian).
segments.s248-g18: چو introduces a comparison/likeness here (‘as/like’); غرم ژیان is a compact poetic phrase meaning ‘the fury/anger of life’ (غرم = wrath, ژیان = life). با تو آیم is literally ‘I come with you/ to you’ where آیم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive used prospectively (‘I will come’), and دوان is an adverbial/participial element ‘running, rushing’ modifying the motion.
segments.s248-g19: شوم is an optative/expressive form (‘may I become / would that I be’) rather than a simple present; پیش means ‘before / in the presence of’ and governs the following name بیژن (Bijan). بغلتم is بغل + ـم, ‘my embrace,’ with the possessive clitic attached, and بخاک is a poetic directional phrase ‘to the ground / onto the earth.’ The clause expresses a wish/curse contingent on the following condition.
segments.s248-g20: مگر here functions as ‘except; unless’ introducing the limiting condition. بازیابم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive ‘I recover / I win back,’ with the explicit subject pronoun من for emphasis. آن کیش پاک is a noun phrase ‘that pure kiš’ where کیش means ‘disposition / character / custom’ and پاک is the adjective ‘pure.’
segments.s248-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ پیغام گرگین is a genitive phrase ‘Gorgin’s message’ (گرگین is the proper name Gorgin). برستم here is the fused preposition+name construction meaning ‘to Rostam’ (بر + رستم), and رسید is the past tense verb ‘reached / arrived.’ The clause thus states a temporal clause: ‘When Gorgin’s message reached Rostam…’
segments.s248-g22: یکی is an indefinite numeral/particle ‘one / a certain’; باد سرد is the noun phrase ‘a cold wind’ (باد = wind, سرد = cold). از + جگر is a poetic source phrase ‘from the liver/heart’ where جگر functions as ‘heart, seat of feeling’; برکشید is a past tense verb ‘lifted / drew up / rose,’ so the image is a cold blast rising from his heart.
segments.s248-g23: بپیچید is a past/formal verb meaning ‘he twisted / he writhed’ (the prefix بـ- here is part of the classical verbal form). ازان درد = ‘from that pain’ (ازان = from that, درد = pain/sorrow), and پیغام اوی is ‘his message’ where اوی is a possessive pronoun (‘his/her’) attached after the noun — a typical older/poetic order.
segments.s248-g24: غم آمدش is a light clause ‘sorrow came to him’ with the clitic ـش on آمد indicating ‘to him.’ ازان qualifies the source (‘from that’), بیهده کام is a two‑word phrase ‘vain/futile desire’ (بیهده = futile, کام = desire), and اوی again is the possessive ‘his,’ so the whole means ‘grief came upon him from that futile longing.’
segments.s248-g9: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه 'gaze/look' + کن 'do!' → 'look!'. بدین is the archaic/poetic equivalent of به این ('at/to this'). گنبد گوژپشت is a noun followed by a postpositive adjective: گنبد 'dome' + گوژپشت 'hunchbacked'. Together: 'Look at this hunchbacked dome.'
segments.s248-g10: که introduces a relative clause ('which'). خیره functions as 'staring/astonished' (a descriptive participle). چراغ دلم را = 'the lamp/light of my heart' with را marking the definite object; note 'دلم' is 'دل + ـم' (my heart). بکشت is a poetically archaic perfective/past verb meaning 'killed' or 'put out' — here 'extinguished'. The clause: 'which, by its stare, extinguished the light of my heart.'
segments.s248-g11: بتاریکی اندر = 'in/into the darkness' (بتاریکی = poetic به تاریکی). مرا is the indirect object 'to me' (tokenized oddly); ره نمود is 'a way/path appeared' (ره 'way' + نمود 'appeared/showed' in past). Word order places the locative phrase first, then the experiencer, then the nominal subject and verb: 'In the darkness a way appeared to me.'
segments.s248-g12: نوشته = 'the writing' or 'inscription'. چنین بود = 'thus was' / 'it was thus.' The triple repetition of بود is a poetic/archaic emphatic frame: the inscription is reported with the formulaic 'thus was (that which was)'. Grammatically, نوشته (noun) is followed by the reported content introduced elliptically and rendered as چنین بود بود آنچ بود = 'it was thus—that which was.'
segments.s248-g13: بر آتش = 'upon/into the fire' (بر + آتش). نهم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative of نهادن/نهفتن used here with an injunctive sense 'I will put/cast' (poetic). خویشتن is the emphatic reflexive 'myself', and پیش شاه = 'in the presence of / before the king' (پیش = before/in front of + شاه 'king'). The clause orders locative + verb + reflexive + prepositional phrase: 'I will cast myself into the fire before the king.'
segments.s248-g14: گر is the conditional 'if'. آمرزش 'forgiveness' is the subject/topic of the clause; آرد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'brings/grants' (poetic form of آورد). مرا is the object 'me' (tokenized oddly), and زین گناه = 'of/from this sin' (زین is poetic contraction of از این). The clause means 'if forgiveness grants me (relief) from this sin' or more naturally 'if forgiveness absolves me of this sin.'
segments.s248-g15: مگر here functions as 'unless'. باز گردَد is a jussive/subjunctive passive-like expression 'may return / be restored' (باز = again/restore + گردد = become/return). ز بد نام من = 'from my bad name' i.e. 'from disgrace / bad reputation' (ز = from; بد نام = bad name/reputation; من = my). The clause reads 'unless (مگر) my name/reputation be restored from its bad state.'
segments.s248-g16: بپیران is a prepositional/benefactive phrase 'to/for the elders' (بـ + پیران). سر is the direct object 'head' (here idiomatic for life/honor). این بد سرانجام من is a genitive/nominal phrase 'this bad/outcome/end of mine' (این = this; بد سرانجام = ill-fated/unhappy ending; من = my). Combined: 'I will lay my head before the elders for this unhappy end of mine.'

sentences.s248-s13: He said to the messenger, "Turn back; tell him, 'O stupefied, impure man.'"
sentences.s248-s14: Have you not heard that tale of the leopard which, in that deep sea, struck the whale?
sentences.s248-s15: For if desire gains the upper hand over reason, no one can get free from the grip of desire.
sentences.s248-s16: The wise man humbled desire; his tale was like a brave lion.
sentences.s248-s29: Tahmatan came and, at the king's request, spread the sun‑like splendour upon the king.
sentences.s248-s30: Gorgin spoke with the king about that lost fortune and the bad state of the times.
sentences.s248-s31: To him the king said, 'O my commander, I warn you and (I) ever break fetters.'
